text.content:
The Aerdy Commercial-Court Network

A loose, unofficial network of Aerdy commercial-court advocates, retired imperial customs officials, and senior firm partners who have been quietly mapping the same conspiracy as Caelith Dunivar — but from a different angle. Their interest is trade, not Yeomanry civic order. They want the route-account practices stopped because the practices have made their own commercial work untrustworthy.

Why this faction matters

Without the Network, the party's only ally is Caelith and his subordinates. That structurally narrows the campaign — every contact, every introduction, every safe house traces back to Yeomanry civic intelligence. The Network gives the party a second base of support that doesn't owe its loyalty to Caelith. The Network's interests overlap with the party's by about 70%. Where they diverge, the party has a moral choice.

The Network's interest

  • The conspiracy has been using Aerdy commercial-court forms and seals to legitimise route-accounts and exception-ledgers. Every time the conspiracy forges a court hand, an advocate's reputation gets contaminated.
  • Several Network members have lost their own clients to the conspiracy's witness-pressure operations.
  • The Network has been quietly compiling evidence for two years. They do not have enough to file. They have not had a partner to share with.
  • The party arrives in Phase 4 with Rel Astra evidence the Network has been needing.

Who's in it

Three principal figures and an outer ring of contacts. The party will meet the principals through Therion Halask (if Path A survived) in Phase 4 or 5; they will encounter Network contacts in passing throughout Phases 5–7.

Principal 1: Mistress Lyra Vellanen

Senior commercial-court advocate, Rel Astra. The Network's effective coordinator. See her dedicated page in this journal.

Principal 2: Pretor Halvard Tess

Retired imperial customs official, now a private commercial arbitrator in Hardby. The Network's quiet money. See his dedicated page.

Principal 3: Senior Magister Olen Drask

Aerdy College of Commercial Law, Rel Astra. The Network's scholar; quietly assembling a published version of their findings that will appear in 580 CY at earliest. See his dedicated page.

The Network's posture

The Network is cautious. They have been mapping the conspiracy for two years without anyone being caught. They will not endanger their slow advantage for a party that hasn't earned trust. The party earns trust by: bringing them evidence they can use, protecting their witnesses, and refusing public action that would compromise the Network's cover.

Where the Network diverges from the party

The Network's goal is to stop the surface route-account practice so that Aerdy commerce can recover. They do not particularly want the party to descend the Seventh Stair. They consider what is below to be not their problem. If the party returns from the deep route with information that would help the Network's case, the Network is happy; if the party never returns, the Network is sorry but does not consider it a failure.

This is the moral edge. Caelith wants the party alive. The Network wants the case proved. The party may have to choose between these.

Mistress Lyra Vellanen

Senior commercial-court advocate, Rel Astra. The Network's coordinator.

Quick read

  • Age: late fifties.
  • Frame: tall, thin, careful. Walks with a deliberation that is not infirmity but is no longer young.
  • Dress: Aerdy commercial dress at the senior level — charcoal wool advocate's coat, white linen at the throat, a small enamelled commercial-court pin (gold-and-jet) at the collar. Reading-glass on a silver chain.
  • Manner: precise, polite, slightly chilly. Speaks in complete sentences. Listens better than she speaks.
  • Stat block: Noble (MM 2024, CR 1/8) with proficiencies in Insight, Persuasion, History, Investigation; treat as effective CR 1 for any social or research-driven encounter.

Motive

Lyra has spent thirty-two years as a commercial-court advocate. She has watched the conspiracy contaminate her profession for the last two. She wants the contamination stopped. She does not want it stopped publicly — that would damage her clients' interests. She wants it stopped quietly, through formal court filings that look like ordinary commercial disputes but cumulatively dismantle the route-account practice.

Tell

When Lyra is hiding information, she gives a slightly fuller answer than necessary. She has trained herself out of brevity-tells. The overcompensation is her tell.

Fear

That the Network is discovered before they file. They have one chance at quiet dismantling. A loud party would force everything into public view and ruin the work.

Leverage

  • Evidence she can use in court filings — this is what she most wants from the party.
  • A promise to keep the Network's existence private — she will give the party real help in exchange.
  • An introduction from Therion Halask — if Path A survived, Halask is her old colleague.

What does not work: bribery (she will laugh, gently), public threat (she will refer the party to her own advocate within the hour), Yeomanry authority brought openly (she will be cordial and useless).

What Lyra can do for the party

  • Aerdy court filings. She can quietly file commercial-court actions against firms the party has implicated. This does not stop the conspiracy but creates legal weight that forces the conspiracy to spend resources defending.
  • Document interpretation. She can read commercial-court forms and seals as accurately as Iren reads manuscripts.
  • Safe house in Rel Astra. An apartment above a wine merchant on Imperial Way. Quiet. Anonymous. The party can use it once.
  • Advocate's witness slip. She can issue a formal slip protecting one Phase 6 or Phase 7 witness (Doras Kemmel, Yorth Pell, Pesh, or others) from Aerdy commercial-court inquiry. Only one; she will not stretch.
  • Introductions. She can introduce the party to Pretor Tess (Hardby) and Magister Drask (Rel Astra College).

What Lyra will not do

  • Endanger the Network's cover.
  • Help the party descend the Seventh Stair.
  • Issue any document that names the deeper faction (she does not know who they are; she will not guess).
  • Speak ill of Caelith Dunivar, with whom she has correspondence she will not show the party.

Useful Lyra lines

  • “You are not the first party that has brought me this kind of evidence. You are the first that has brought it before I needed to ask.”
  • “I do not want a public verdict. I want a long, careful, quiet erosion of the practice that has been ruining my profession.”
  • “If you go below, I will mourn you. I will not stop you. I cannot follow.”

The exchange

By Phase 7, the party should have offered Lyra at least two pieces of evidence: a copy of the Ashen Itinerary (or a fragment), and Vone's route-exception ledger excerpt. In return, Lyra has provided: a court-issued witness protection slip (used on Doras or whichever K&R staff turned), an Aerdy advocate's authorisation that lets the party access the Guild archive without raising Heat, and a quiet message to Halask (Phase 4) that confirms what Halask suspected. The exchange is not a transaction; it is a partnership.

Pretor Halvard Tess

Retired imperial customs official. Now a private commercial arbitrator in Hardby. The Network's quiet money.

Quick read

  • Age: sixty-two.
  • Frame: heavy in the shoulders, soft in the face. Greying beard kept short. Walks with a cane he doesn't strictly need.
  • Dress: Aerdi merchant-class formal — a dark green wool coat with brass buttons, white linen, a thin gold watch-chain. A small retired imperial customs pin (a stylised sailing ship behind a balance) at his collar; he is entitled to it but no one stops him in the street for it.
  • Manner: warmer than Lyra, slower to commit. Likes the party more easily; trusts them more carefully.
  • Stat block: Noble (MM 2024, CR 1/8) with Investigation, History, Persuasion proficiencies.

Motive

Tess retired from imperial customs five years ago after declining to certify three shipments that were not, in his professional opinion, what they claimed to be. He took a generous pension. He has been paying for the Network's quiet activities out of that pension and his arbitrator's fees. His motive is professional: he believes the route-account practice has poisoned the commercial-law tradition he served.

What Tess can do for the party

  • Funds. He cannot give the party large sums openly but can quietly absorb their expenses with Iren, Vone, and the Guild — making it look like the party paid retail.
  • Hardby contacts. He knows the senior partners of every commercial firm in Hardby personally. If the party needs to bypass a hostile clerk, Tess can make a phone call (or its 569 CY equivalent — a courier-and-card system) on their behalf.
  • Customs intelligence. He has retired but still has friends in customs. He can give the party old shipping manifests that show the conspiracy's earlier surface footprint.

Tess's particular vulnerability

His son, Halvard Tess the Younger, is a journeyman commercial-court clerk in Greyhawk City. If the conspiracy notices the Network's interest, they may apply pressure to the son. Tess will eventually tell the party this; he wants them to know he has a stake.

Useful Tess line

“I am not your friend, exactly. I am the person who is paying for you to do something I cannot do myself. That makes us colleagues. It will have to be enough.”

pages.name: Senior Magister Olen Drask — Network Scholar

text.content:
Senior Magister Olen Drask

Aerdy College of Commercial Law, Rel Astra. The Network's scholar.

Quick read

  • Age: late seventies.
  • Frame: small, slight, very upright. Bald with a fringe of white. Cold blue eyes.
  • Dress: full academic dress when teaching (heavy black gown, white linen bands); ordinary advocate's coat otherwise.
  • Manner: scholarly. Cordial without warmth. Speaks to the party as if they were promising students.
  • Stat block: Mage Apprentice (MM 2024, CR 2) with cantrips and one prepared 1st-level slot; combat is a misuse of him.

Motive

Drask has been quietly assembling a scholarly monograph titled “Route-Account Practice in Late Aerdy Commercial Law: Towards a Closure.” It is forty thousand words long. He intends to publish it in 580 CY, ten years after the conspiracy's surface practices have been administratively dismantled. The publication is his weapon. He believes academic publication, properly timed, can prevent the practice from returning for another century.

What Drask can do for the party

  • Historical context. He can read any commercial-court document the party brings him and place it in the longer history of Aerdy administrative practice.
  • The Class VII glossary. He has a copy of the same glossary Iren has on her highest shelf — with three additional pages Iren's copy is missing.
  • Access to the College's restricted archive. With his signature, the party can read sealed monographs on missing surveyors going back to 487 CY.

Useful Drask line

“Stopping the practice now is a service to the present. Documenting it after is a service to the future. I am writing for the future. You are working for the present. We are not the same. We are also not opponents.”

pages.name: Where the Network appears in each phase

text.content:
Where the Network appears in each phase

Phase 1

Background only. A footnote in Caelith's brief mentions “quieter Aerdy advocates” who have inquired about Sereth-adjacent matters in the past year. He doesn't name them.

Phase 2

A formal Aerdy commercial-court inquiry letter has arrived at C&V Loftwick from Mistress Vellanen's office. It is innocuous in tone; it is asking after the firm's record-keeping practices in a way that is technically routine. If the party recovers C&V correspondence in Phase 2 they may see this letter.

Phase 3

In Hardby, if the party visits a commercial-arbitrator's office, they may meet Pretor Tess in passing. He is cordial. He does not introduce himself as Network. He listens.

Phase 4 — first contact

If Path A (Sereth indicted) was followed and Halask is alive and active, Halask introduces the party to Mistress Vellanen during the post-indictment proceedings. This is the formal first contact. Vellanen is cautious. She does not commit. She asks for one piece of evidence the party can spare. If they give it, she signals that she will be in touch.

If Path B (private removal) was used, the Network is colder but still reachable. Halask cannot openly introduce. The party must seek Vellanen out themselves through Tess.

If Path C (kinetic removal) was used, the Network distances itself. Lyra will eventually meet the party in Phase 6 or 7 if Tess vouches; not before.

Phase 5

Tess offers to absorb the party's Greyhawk-area expenses anonymously, freeing them to spend on Vone and Iren without depleting reserves. Drask sends a single sealed envelope to Caelith with a marginal annotation Caelith will not show the party but will use in his subsequent letters.

Phase 6

Vellanen's office issues the Doras Kemmel witness-protection slip (if Doras turned). The slip works. K&R's owner in Hardby is enraged but cannot retaliate without exposing his own connections.

Phase 7

Drask provides the Class VII glossary's three missing pages, which contain the explicit Aerdy administrative classification of “permitted descents.” The pages do not name the deep faction but confirm the route-class as historical and intentional.

By Phase 7's end, Vellanen has filed seven commercial-court actions against firms and individuals the party has implicated. The actions are quiet, technical, and devastating. The conspiracy's surface infrastructure begins to crumble at the same time the party reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn.

Phase 8 (and beyond)

The Network cannot follow the party below. They will keep working on the surface. When the party returns, the Network will have a different conversation with them than Caelith will. The Network will want what they learned. They will not want to know how it was learned.
